coredata.dc:title: Carbonized nitrogen doped carbon dots functionalized PbO2 electrode for efficient degradation of organic pollutants
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Wenlong
Wu, Yuanfeng
Yi, Guiyun
Guo, Xiangkun
Yin, Yue
Xu, Bin
Zhang, Jinfang
Sun, Lutong
Zeng, Huihui
Xing, Baolin
Zhang, Yulong
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) underwent carbonization at different temperature (0, 400, 600, 800, 1000), further applied to coat at surface of Ti/SnO2-Sb2O3 sheet for the preparation of NCDs-T interlayer. Subsequently, the electrodeposition method was applied to prepare the NCDs-T/PbO2 electrodes, the electrocatalytic performance of which were investigated in degradation of methylene blue (MB). The prepared NCDs-T/PbO2 electrodes were characterized by SEM, XRD, XPS, CV, SCV, and EIS. Interestingly, the degradation efficiency of the NCDs-600/PbO2 electrode was observed with only 5 % decrease after 10 cycles, indicating that the introduction of the NCDs intermediate layer could enhance the stability of the PbO2 electrode. Importantly, the highest degradation rate of methylene blue was to be 91.6 % (180 min) when the degradation process was performed at pH=5, the current density of 30 mA cm−2, and 100 mg L −1 (MB solution) in the presence of NCDs-800/PbO2 electrodes for 3 h. Besides, the removal rate of TOC was up to 50.3 %, showing a higher mineralization efficiency compared with pure PbO2 electrode, which was related to higher oxygen precipitation over potential and better conductivity of NCDs-600/PbO2 electrodes. Moreover, ∙OH as the primary active species was identified in the electrocatalytic oxidation process. Furthermore, a possible degradation mechanism for MB over NCDs-800/PbO2 electrodes was proposed, and further demonstrated by DFT calculations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-CDs-T/PbO2
Methylene blue
Electrocatalytic oxidation
Degradation mechanism
DFT calculations

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Performance analysis of NCDs-T/PbO2 electrode electrocatalytic degradation of methylene blue

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD spectrum of PbO2 and NCDs-800/PbO2 electrode.

SEM image of (a) NCDs, (b) NCDs after carbonization at 800 °C, (c)PbO2 electrode, and (d) NCDs-800/PbO2 electrode; (e) Elemental distribution of NCDs-800/PbO2 electrode; (f) EDS of NCDs-800/PbO2 electrode; (g) Particle size distribution statistics of (b).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy of PbO2 and NCDs-800/PbO2 electrode; (a) full survey; (b) C 1 s spectrum of NCDs-800/PbO2 electrode; (c) High resolution O1s spectrum of PbO2 electrode; (d) High resolution O1s spectrum of NCDs-800/PbO2 electrode.
(a) CV curves, (b) SCV curves and (c) EIS curves and equivalent circuit diagram of PbO2 and NCDs-800/PbO2 electrode in 0.2 M Na2SO4 solution.

(a) Electrochemical degradation of MB for 180 min over PbO2 and NCDs-600/PbO2 electrode; (b) Total organic carbon (TOC) removal rate of PbO2 and NCDs-600/PbO2 electrode; (c) Effect of carbonization temperature on degradation of MB by NCDs-T/PbO2 electrode; (d) kinetic fit curves. Reaction conditions: 300 mL of methylene blue solution (100 mg L −1), an initial pH of 5, and a current density of 30 mA cm−2.

The Effect of (a) initial MB concentrations, (b) current density, and (c) initial pH on degradation of MB by NCDs-800/PbO2 electrode; (d) fitting kinetic curves of (a); (e) fitting kinetic curves of (b); (f) fitting kinetic curves of (c). The optimized reaction conditions: 300 mL of methylene blue solution (100 mg L −1), an initial pH of 5, and a current density of 30 mA cm−2.

(a) the effect of TBA on electrocatalytic degradation of MB; (b) Reaction mechanism of MB electrocatalytic degradation; HOMO orbits of (c) carbonized NCDs and (d) PbO2 (1 0 1); Excited state of (e) carbonized NCDs, (f) PbO2 (1 0 1), (h) the hybrid model structured with the PbO2 and the carbonized NCDs, where yellow region represents holes and the blue region represents electrons. (g) Charge density of the hybrid model structured with PbO2 and the carbonized NCDs.
The process for the synthesis of carbonized NCDs-T/PbO2 electrode.
Experimental apparatus for electrocatalytic degradation of organic pollutant.

head.ce:title: Carbonized nitrogen doped carbon dots functionalized PbO2 electrode for efficient degradation of organic pollutants

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Key Laboratory of Coal Green Conversion, International Jiont Laboratory of Coal Clean Utilization, Collaborative Innovation Center of Coal Work Safety and Clean High Efficiency Utilization, and College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo 454003, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Key Laboratory of Coal Green Conversion
International Jiont Laboratory of Coal Clean Utilization
Collaborative Innovation Center of Coal Work Safety and Clean High Efficiency Utilization, and College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henan Key Laboratory of Coal Green Conversion, International Jiont Laboratory of Coal Clean Utilization, Collaborative Innovation Center of Coal Work Safety and Clean High Efficiency Utilization, and College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo 454003, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) underwent carbonization at different temperature (0, 400, 600, 800, 1000), further applied to coat at surface of Ti/SnO2-Sb2O3 sheet for the preparation of NCDs-T interlayer. Subsequently, the electrodeposition method was applied to prepare the NCDs-T/PbO2 electrodes, the electrocatalytic performance of which were investigated in degradation of methylene blue (MB). The prepared NCDs-T/PbO2 electrodes were characterized by SEM, XRD, XPS, CV, SCV, and EIS. Interestingly, the degradation efficiency of the NCDs-600/PbO2 electrode was observed with only 5 % decrease after 10 cycles, indicating that the introduction of the NCDs intermediate layer could enhance the stability of the PbO2 electrode. Importantly, the highest degradation rate of methylene blue was to be 91.6 % (180 min) when the degradation process was performed at pH=5, the current density of 30 mA cm−2, and 100 mg L −1 (MB solution) in the presence of NCDs-800/PbO2 electrodes for 3 h. Besides, the removal rate of TOC was up to 50.3 %, showing a higher mineralization efficiency compared with pure PbO2 electrode, which was related to higher oxygen precipitation over potential and better conductivity of NCDs-600/PbO2 electrodes. Moreover, ∙OH as the primary active species was identified in the electrocatalytic oxidation process. Furthermore, a possible degradation mechanism for MB over NCDs-800/PbO2 electrodes was proposed, and further demonstrated by DFT calculations.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: The PbO2 electrode has been reported with high catalytic activity [1] and stability due to its crystal structure with non-stoichiometric ratio of about 1.95 ∼ 1.98[2], and electrons easily transferred between oxygen vacancies [3]. Compared with SnO2 electrode, PbO2 electrode has higher electrical conductivity and service life [4], and PbO2 has been used both as an electrode for lead batteries [5] and as an anode material for electrocatalytic oxidation of organic pollutants [6].

Generally, PbO2 has two crystal types including rhombohedral (α-PbO2)[7] and tetragonal (β-PbO2)[8]. For α-PbO2, metal ions (Pb) are located at the eight vertices and centers, and its crystalline particles are coarse [9], with poor electrical conductivity and good mechanical strength, while β-PbO2 metal ions are located at the centers of the faces in the deformed octahedron in an ordered and compact structure [10], with high electrical conductivity [11] and precipitation of oxygen over-potential. Recently, PbO2 electrodes have garnered significant interest due to their high oxygen precipitation over potential, robust corrosion resistance [12], low cost [13], ease of synthesis, and other favorable characteristics, making them a promising choice as an anode material [14].

Oxygen precipitation over potential of PbO2 is in the range of 1.8 ∼ 2.0 V [15], which is higher than that of standard hydrogen electrodes. The wide potential range can make the electrode with the ability to reduce the occurrence of side reactions [16], such as electrolysis of water to produce oxygen, and the high current efficiency also improves the degradation rate of pollutants. PbO2 with good electrochemical performance [17] results in the direct degradation of pollutants on the surface of its crystal structure [18]. Importantly, the mass transfer process as the key step is susceptibly effected during low concentration [19], and ·OH generated from the surface of PbO2 was determined as the active species for pollutant degradation [20]. In addition, PbO2 has greater mineralization efficiency for organic pollutants compared with other electrodes [21], which is due to the fact that ·OH produced by electrolysis of water is weakly adsorbed on the surface of the anode [22], and such ·OH is more efficiently interacted with the organic pollutants than the strongly adsorbed ·OH [23].

However, internal stress within β-PbO2 became obviously increasing during the preparation process, and electrodeposited β-PbO2 was easy to fall off from the substrate [24]. As a result, various methods have been adopted to construct the intermediate layer for achieving the highly stable electrodes [25]. For example, Duan et al. [26]. combined electrophoretic deposition and electrodeposition techniques to prepare a new type of PbO2 electrode with graphene nanosheets interlayer (GNS-PbO2), and the results showed that the service life of the GNS-PbO2 electrode (107.9 h) was 1.93 times longer than that of the conventional PbO2 electrode (55.9 h). Gui et al. [27]. used the anodic electrodeposition method to prepare a ZnO/PEG (polyethylene glycol)-Co(II)-PbO2 nanocomposite electrodes, and it was found that the introduction of the ZnO intermediate layer could not only alleviate the electrical distortion between the substrate and the plating layer, but also improve the cracking problem of the plating layer, and the stability and catalytic activity of the electrodes were significantly improved. Ansari et al. [28]. prepared Ti/SnO2-Sb/β-PbO2 anode using SnO2-Sb interlayer by cathodic deposition and calcination. The results showed that the addition of SnO2-Sb interlayer has increased the oxygen precipitation over potential of the electrode by 0.45 V, and the service life by 60 h. Moreover, the degradation rate of dinitrobenzene and COD removal reached 100 % and 98 %, respectively. These findings indicate that the introduction of an intermediate layer can indeed improve the stability and the electrocatalytic performance of PbO2 electrodes.

Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) are noted for their superior fluorescence and are extensively used in fluorescent probes. For example, Atchudan et al. have synthesized NCDs with high durability and quantum yield from various natural sources, such as Phyllanthus acidus [29], dwarf banana peels [30], and betel leaves [31], and the NCDs were also attempted as the probe for detecting Fe3+ ions, live-cell imaging, and fluorescent inks. Besides, Krishnaiah et al. [32]. also synthesized NCDs from Kentucky bluegrass for detecting Mn2+ and Fe3+ ions. However, NCDs have not been widely used in electrocatalysis at present. Moreover, the excellent conductivity and stability [33,34] also made NCDs be efficiently used as an intermediate layer for the synthesis of multicomponent electrodes.

In this study, PbO2 electrodes were prepared by electrodeposition method [35], further identified by nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) for the synthesis of NCDs-T/PbO2 electrodes. Besides, the effects of carbonization temperature on the electrode microscopic morphology, crystal structure, surface properties, and electrocatalytic activity were respectively studied. Moreover, the proposed mechanism for the enhanced organic pollutant degradation on the electrocatalytic process was also validated by DFT calculations. The results showed that NCDs-T/PbO2 electrode could efficiently degrade organic pollutants with high precipitation oxygen overpotential and conductivity as well as excellent stability and electrocatalytic performance. Therefore, NCDs-T/PbO2 electrode has a broad application prospect in the electrocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants.

The process for the synthesis of carbonized NCDs-T/PbO2 electrode was shown in Scheme 1 .
(1) Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs)

According to the previous report [36], nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were synthesized by microwave method. Citric acid (3 g) and urea (2.8134 g) with molar ratio of 1:1 were dissolved in 60 mL of N, N-dimethylformamide solution. Subsequently, the mixed solution was heated at 180 °C for 30 min. After the solution was completely cooled down to room temperature, the reacted solution was transferred into an MD55 dialysis bag to exclude the reactants. Subsequently, the carbon dot solution was freeze-dried for obtaining the carbon dot powder.
(2) Preparation of carbon dots interlayer

The prepared carbon dots were carbonized at different temperatures (0 °C, 400 °C, 600 °C, 800 °C, 1000 °C) under nitrogen atmosphere. 50 mg of carbonized carbon dots was distributed into 500 µL of anhydrous ethanol. After being stirred for 8 h, 100 µL of DuPont film solution was added into above mixture, and reacted for another 24 h. Furthermore, the carbon dots solution was added dropwise onto the surface of Ti/SnO2-Sb2O3[37], and then pushed flat with a glass rod. Finally, the coated titanium plates were dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 10 h.
(3) Synthesis of carbonized NCDs/PbO2 electrodes
Firstly, 250 mL electrolyte was prepared with the components of 0.2 M Pb(NO3)2 and 0.01 M NaF [38], and the solution pH was adjusted to 2 by using nitric acid.

During the electroplating process, Ti/SnO2-Sb2O3/NCDs-T was used as the anode and a titanium plate was used as the cathode, and the current of 30 mA cm−2 was continuously output for 60 min. The prepared electrodes were named NCDs-T/PbO2 (T = 0, 400, 600, 800, 1000).
The electrocatalytic activity of NCDs-T/PbO2 electrodes was determined by degradation of Methylene blue (MB), carried out in a 500 mL glass vessel (Scheme 2 ).

300 mL MB solution including 0.1 M Na2SO4 was used as electrolyte, and the pH of which was altered to be 5 via nitric acid. Meanwhile, the prepared modified PbO2 electrode (3 cm×5 cm) was used as anode while the pretreated titanium plate (3 cm×5 cm) was used as cathode, and the distance between the two electrodes was set to 3 cm. The degradation experiment was carried out at current density of 30 mA cm−2 for 180 min at room temperature, and the solution was detected every 30 min [39] by UV spectrophotometer(UV-3600PLUS).
The crystal structure of the as-prepared electrode was characterized by X-ray diffractometer (D8 Advance, Bruker, Cu-Kα), and the data was recorded in the range of 2θ = 10 ∼ 80°
The micro-morphology and structure of the electrode material was determined by the scanning electron microscope (JSM-6390LV model of Nippon Electron Corporation).

A Thermo ESCALAB 250 XI (Thermo Corporation, USA, Al-K α-rays) type photoelectron spectrometer was used to study the chemical valence states of Pb, C, O, and N elements. All the spectra was calibrated according to C 1 s peak at 284.80 eV.

All electrochemical properties were tested using a three-electrode system with a CHI760E electrochemical workstation, of which a platinum sheet electrode (1 cm × 1 cm) was used as the counter electrode, and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) was used as the reference electrode as well as 0.2 M Na2SO4 was severed as electrolyte. The voltage test range of cyclic voltammetry curve (CV) was set in the range of 0 ∼ 2 V (100 mV s −1), and the voltage range of steady state polarization curve (SCV) was studied at 0 ∼ 3 V. Besides, the frequency range of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was 105 ∼ 0.1 Hz, with an amplitude of 5 mV sinusoidal wave.

The CP2K2023.1 code was applied for geometric optimization and energy at the level of Perdew–Burke–Ernzerh (PBE) method equipped with DZVP-MOLOPT-SR-GTH basic sets. A 400 R y plane wave cutoff for the auxiliary grid was set. A Broyden mixing method was applied to accelerate the convergence to the requested total energy threshold value of 10−6 Hartree for the total energy difference between two consecutive SCF iteration steps. Atomic coordinates have also been provided in the end of the document.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra of PbO2 and NCDs-800/PbO2 electrodes were shown in Fig. 1 , in which the three labeled peaks correspond to β-PbO2, α-PbO2 and Pb2O3, respectively. β-PbO2 exists four major diffraction peaks (110), (101), (211), and (301) [40,41], which are located at 25.7°, 32.0°, 49.1°, and 62.5°, respectively. The appearance of (111), (002), (021), and (113) crystal facets confirms the presence of α-PbO2 within the electrode, which has the disadvantages of rough particles and poor catalytic activity. The diffraction intensity of the (101) crystal plane of α-PbO2 obviously decreased, while the (002), (021), and (113) crystal planes disappeared for the NCDs-800/PbO2 electrode, which indicated that the introduction of the NCDs can inhibit the growth of α-PbO2, and simultaneously induce the growth of β-PbO2 crystals along the (101) crystal plane. The average grain size of β-PbO2 crystals was calculated using Debye-Scherrer formula, and the average grain sizes of PbO2 and NCDs-800/PbO2 electrodes were to be 21.79 nm and 20.65 nm, respectively, indicating that the introduction of NCDs did not change the average grain size of the crystals.

The microstructure of as-synthesized samples were characterized by scanning electron microscope (Fig. 2 ). As shown in Fig. 2a, the particles of carbon dots synthesized by microwave method are inhomogeneous in shape. Hence, vigorous synthesis reaction conditions as well as the short synthesis time (30 min) maybe account for the uneven size of carbon dots, and various groups such as hydroxyl, and carboxyl linking to the surface structure make the carbon dots with poor electrical conductivity. When the NCDs underwent carbonization in an atmosphere of nitrogen at 800 °C, the surface of the carbon dots become homogeneous in structure, and the corresponding electrical conductivity is drastically improved due to the removal of electronegative groups from the surface of the carbon dots under high temperature. Besides, the PbO2 particles are pyramidal in structure (Fig. 2c), and the pyramidal shape was found with uneven particle size. Moreover, certain gaps and cracks on the particle body were also observed between the particles, which could lead to electrolyte corrosion of the titanium substrate and passivation of the electrodes as well as the short electrode's life [42]. As shown in Fig. 2d, the cracks on the surface of PbO2 electrodes were obviously reduced after being modified by NCDs-800, and the carbon dots were adsorbed onto the electrode as an intermediate layer, which made the PbO2 have the ability to offer more active sites, to improve the electron transfer, and to strengthen the service life of the electrode. The element distribution and energy spectrum of the NCDs-800/PbO2 electrode were shown in Fig. 2e, and Fig. 2f, and the presence of four kinds of elements including C, O, N, and Pb were confirmed by energy spectrum, and also observed within the surface of NCDs-800/PbO2 electrode, which suggested that the NCDs-800 have been successfully deposited on the PbO2 electrode. Fig. 2g shows the particle size distribution statistics of NCDs after carbonization at 800 °C, and the particle size is mainly distributed in the vicinity of 800 nm, and its average particle size is calculated to be 810 nm. Compared with uncarbonized NCDs (Fig. 2a), the carbonized NCDs were found with smaller particles, implied that the carbonized NCDs would have a larger specific surface, and offer more active sites for organic pollutants adsorption.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the PbO2 and NCDs-800/PbO2 electrodes were exhibited in Fig. 3 . The appearance of C 1 s peaks and N 1 s peaks in the full spectra of NCDs-800/PbO2 indicates the successful loading of NCDs onto the surface of PbO2 electrodes. In addition, the C1s spectrum was deconvoluted into three symmetrical peaks corresponding to CC, CO, and C = O bonds, of which CC bond with the electrode is the most favorable for electron transport. The changes in the chemical state of the O element between PbO2 and NCDs-800/PbO2 were observed in Fig. 3c and d, and the O 1 s high-resolution spectra of the pure PbO2 electrode are divided into three peaks at the positions of 529.0 eV, 531.0 eV, and 532.5 eV, which represent the binding energy of the lattice oxygen (OL), the adsorbed hydroxyl oxygen (OOH), and the adsorbed oxygen in water (OH2O), respectively. The proportions of the three forms of oxygen were calculated based on the integral areas of the three peaks, in which the proportion of OOH at the NCDs-800/PbO2 electrode was 58.34 %, which was higher than that at the pure PbO2 electrode (55.45 %). It is well known that OOH plays an important role in electrocatalytic oxidation [43], and electrodes with a high OOH content are considered to be more easier to produce ·OH, as a result, the NCDs-T/PbO2 electrodes would show a higher mineralization efficiency.

Fig. 4 a shows the CV curves of PbO2 and NCDs-800/PbO2 electrodes measured in 0.2 M Na2SO4 solution with a scanning rate is 100 mV s −1, and the PbO2 electrode shows an anodic peak at 1.43 V and a cathodic peak at 0.89 V, corresponding to the interconversion of Pb2+ and Pb4+. The increased oxidation peak current of the NCDs-800/PbO2 electrode indicates that the introduction of NCDs could enhance the catalytic activity of the electrode. In addition, the integral area is proportional to the voltammetric charge, and the voltammetric charge q* of NCDs-800/PbO2 electrode was 0.0628 C, which was higher than that of PbO2 electrode (0.0593 C), indicating that there were more active sites on the surface of NCDs-800/PbO2 electrode [44]. Fig. 4b shows the SCV curves of PbO2 and NCDs-800/PbO2 measured in 0.2 M Na2SO4 solution, the oxygen precipitation over potentials of the pure PbO2 electrode and the NCDs-800/PbO2 electrode were 1.77 V and 1.86 V vs. SCE, respectively. Generally, oxygen evolution reaction (OER) belongs to the side reaction in the catalytic degradation process [45], and the high or low level of the oxygen precipitation over potential affects the current utilization efficiency. Besides, the lower oxygen precipitation over potential induces the combination of ·OH to produce oxygen, which reduces the number of active oxides. This is detrimental to the degradation reaction. The utilization of NCDs-T as the intermediate layer could improve the oxygen evolution potential (OEP) of PbO2, and thus the NCDs-T/PbO2 electrode possesses better electrocatalytic oxidizing ability [46].

Fig. 4c shows the electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of the PbO2 and NCDs-800/PbO2 electrodes. The EIS process includes electrochemical and concentration-differential polarization, also confirmed the more complex Faraday impedance of the electrode [47]. In the high-frequency portion of the process is the capacitive arc of the bilayer, while in the low-frequency portion, diffusion control overrode the electrochemical control and Warburg impedance also occurred [48]. The inset in Fig. 4c is the equivalent circuit diagram obtained by simulation with Zview software, where Rs is the electrolyte resistance, Rct is the resistance of the bilayer, ZW is the Warburg impedance, and CPE refers to the capacitance. The bilayer resistance values were calculated to be 392.9 Ω (PbO2) and 7.1 Ω (NCDs-800/PbO2), suggested that the introduction of the NCDs-T could greatly improve the conductivity of the electrode, and the catalytic activity of the electrode was also enhanced.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance analysis of NCDs-T/PbO2 electrode electrocatalytic degradation of methylene blue

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 a shows the reusability of PbO2 and NCDs-600/PbO2 electrodes in degradation of MB. Interestingly, the catalytic performance of the two electrodes maintained a constant in the first five degradation cycles, and the degradation rate of NCDs-600/PbO2 electrode (about 91 %) was higher compared with that of pure PbO2 electrode (about 88 %). For the last five experiments, the degradation rate of the pure PbO2 electrode decayed rapidly to 75 %, which was due to the detachment of the PbO2 coating and the erosion of the titanium substrate by the electrolyte [49]. On the contrary, the degradation rate of NCDs-600/PbO2 electrode only decreased by 5 %, indicating that the addition of NCDs enhanced the interaction between the substrate and the active layer, and titanium substrate was also protected. As a result, NCDs-T/PbO2 electrode was stable during the degradation of MB. In addition, the surface morphology of the recovered NCDs-600/PbO2 was determined by scanning electron microscopy (Fig. S4). After 10 cycles, NCDs-600 was still uniformly distributed in the PbO2 electrode, and no obvious cracks appeared on the surface of the PbO2 electrode, which indicated that the prepared composite electrode was basically undamaged during the electrocatalytic process.

To evaluate the removal of methylene blue and its intermediates from wastewater by NCDs-600/PbO2 electrodes, the reacted solution were tested using a total organic carbon analyzer (Fig. 5b). The results showed that the TOC removal rate of MB over the NCDs-600/PbO2 electrode was 50.3 %, the energy consumption was 66.81 kWh L −1, and the mineralization efficiency was 4.06 %, which significantly improved the mineralization efficiency of the MB compared with that of the pure PbO2 electrode (Table S2). Interestingly, the reduced energy consumption indicated that the construction of the NCDs intermediate layer enhanced the catalytic activity and conductivity of the electrode. Therefore, the NCDs-600/PbO2 electrode has a great advantage in MB degradation.

Fig. 5c showed the effect of various NCDs-T/PbO2 electrodes on the degradation of methylene blue. From the Fig. 5c and d, as the carbonization temperature increased from 0 °C to 1000 °C, the corresponding methylene blue degradation rate increased from 87.0 % to 92.0 %. The reaction rate constant of the NCDs-1000/PbO2 electrode was 14.03×10−3 min−1 (Table S3), which was higher than that of the pure PbO2 electrode (11.97×10−3 min−1) and NCDs-0/PbO2 electrode (11.33×10−3 min−1). The high removal rate was due to the decomposition of some functional groups on the surface of NCDs during carbonization, which resulted in enhancement of electron transport capacity. When the carbonization temperature was 800 °C, the degradation rate of methylene blue was 91.6 %, very close to 92.0 % (1000 °C), implied that the groups of NCDs had been completely removed above 800 °C. Therefore, NCDs-800/PbO2 was used for the subsequent experiments.

The effect of initial MB concentration on the degradation rate at room temperature was shown in Fig. 6 a. When the MB concentration was altered from 50 to 200 mg L −1, the corresponding MB degradation rates reached 93.7 % (50 mg/L), 91.6 %(100 mg/L), 85.1 %(140 mg/L), and 77.7 %(200 mg/L), respectively. In addition, the kinetics constant k for 50 mg L −1 MB concentration was 15.36×10−3 min−1 (Fig. 6d, and Table S4), which was 1.84 times higher than that for 200 mg L −1 MB concentration (8.34×10 −3 min−1). Methylene blue easily removed at low concentration was attributed to the fact that the degradation process was controlled by the diffusion process of ·OH at the electrode surface. In the early stage of degradation, methylene blue and its intermediates diffused to the reaction interface, while the generation rate of ·OH at the electrode surface was greater than the diffusion rate of methylene blue, resulting in the continuous mineralization of methylene blue. As the reaction proceeded, methylene blue continued to be transferred to the electrode and formed an accumulation, and the intermediate products were also continuously generated, which resulted in a competitive reaction between the intermediate products and methylene blue. As a result, MB solution of 100 mg/L was appropriate for the present reaction system.

It has been reported that the generation rate of ·OH is related to the magnitude of current density [50], and the effect of current density was determined in Fig. 6b. The degradation rate of MB increased with the increase of current density (10, 20, 30, 40 mA cm−2), and the degradation rate of MB reached 93.5 % at the current density of 40 mA cm−2, corresponding to the largest reaction kinetic constant (15.19×10−3 min−1, Fig. 6e, and Table S5). Although the high current density is beneficial for ·OH generation, excessive current density can contribute to the occurrence of oxygen precipitation side reactions, which resulted in a decrease in the current efficiency. The current density of 30 mA cm−2 has a higher MB removal rate (91.6 %) and lower energy consumption (66.81 kWh L −1). Hence, 30 mA cm−2 was chosen as the optimal current density for degradation reaction.

The initial pH of the electrolytic solution is the key factor affecting the pollutant removal rate. As shown in Fig. 6c, the initial pH of methylene blue solution was respectively altered to 3, 5, 7, 9 and 11, and the corresponding MB removal rates were to be 85.7 %, 91.6 %, 86.2 %, 81.0 % and 75.4 %, respectively. Fig. 6f shows the fitted kinetic curves at different pH, and the reaction rate constant at pH=5 is 13.76×10−3 min−1 (Table S6), which is higher compared with that at pH=9 (9.23×10−3 min−1) and 11 (7.79×10−3 min−1). The highest removal rate of methylene blue under weakly acidic conditions is due to the reduction of the oxygen precipitation over potential of water from 2.4 V under acidic conditions to 1.23 V under alkaline conditions, which enhances the oxygen precipitation reaction of electrolyzed water and ultimately leads to a decrease in the current efficiency. However, the PbO2 electrode was seriously damaged under strong acidic conditions, leading to the precipitation of Pb ions, which not only affects the service life of the electrode, but also leads to secondary pollution. Hence, pH=5 was optimal for NCDs-800/PbO2 catalyzing reaction system.

To determine whether ·OH is the main catalytically active species in the MB degradation process, tertiary butyl alcohol (TBA) was applied as the ·OH trapping agent [51]. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the degradation rate of MB decreased from 85 % to 5 % when the addition of TBA increased from 1 mM to 20 mM, respectively, which indicated that the addition of TBA could inhibit the catalytic oxidation reaction. As a result, ·OH was the main active substance in the electrocatalytic degradation of MB. The quenching mechanism has been reported as follows: the hydroxyl hydrogen of TBA was seized by free radicals to form tertiary butyl radicals, which was stable due to the hyper-conjugation effect of alkyl groups [52,53].

Since ·OH is the main active substance in the electrocatalytic degradation of methylene blue [54], one possible MB degradation mechanism was proposed in Fig. 7b. Firstly, methylene blue molecules were adsorbed on the surface of NCDs-800/PbO2 electrode. Then, the absorbed MB molecules underwent redox reaction to form intermediate products on the anode surface. Simultaneously, the hydrogen ions were reduced to generate hydrogen gas on the cathode surface (The expression of hydrogen evolution reaction is shown in Eq. (1)). PbO2 as an inactive electrode can act as an inert electron sink to make the water molecule discharge and form the hydroxyl radicals (Eq. (2)). Afterwards, methylene blue molecules were gradually decomposed into small molecular compounds such as benzaldehyde, benzoic acid and ammonia, which are relatively harmless and easily biodegradable. Finally, the small molecule compounds produced by the decomposition of methylene blue can be further degraded by microorganisms, which eventually converted into inorganic substances such as CO2, H2O (Eq. (3))[55].
(1)
2
H
+
+ 2
e
−
→
H 2
(2)
N − C D s − 800 / P b
O 2
+
H 2
O → N − C D s − 800 / P b
O 2
(
· O H
)
+
H
+
+
e
−
(3)
N − C D s − 800 / P b
O 2
(
· O H
)
+ M B → N − C D s − 800 / P b
O 2
+ C
O 2
+
H 2
O

To further unveil the degradation mechanism of MB over the NCDs-T/PbO2 electrode, DFT calculations were performed with the results shown in Figs. 7c-h. According to the previous literatures [56,57], the band gap can be calculated reference to the values of HOMO and LUMO orbits, and the results were listed in Table S7 and Fig. S3. The carbonized NCDs was confirmed with semiconductor properties according to its band gap (1.92 eV), while PbO2 was assigned to conductor due to the narrow band gap. Fig. 7c and Fig. 7e showed the HOMO and the excited state of the carbonized nitrogen-doped carbon dots, and ground state electrons around HOMO can be transferred to PbO2, and the newly-formed holes can also receive the electrons from the decomposed pollutants. Fig. 7d shows the HOMO of β-PbO2, and the number of extra-nuclear electrons of the oxygen atom in the PbO2 molecule has not yet reached the saturation state, which has a strong ability to absorb electrons. According to the theory of radiation-free jump, the electrons adsorbed by PbO2 from water molecules can jump from the ground state to the excited state (Fig. 7f), and further be transferred to the external power. Simultaneously, the water molecules with the loss of electrons underwent decomposition and formed the hydroxyl radicals and the hydrogen ions. The charge density of the hybrid model structured with PbO2 and the carbonized NCDs was shown in Fig. 7g, which was mainly distributed on the surface of hydrogen, carbon, oxygen, nitrogen atoms. However, the electrons were transferred from carbonized NCDs to PbO2 once the hybrid structure was in excited state (Fig. 7h), indicating that the electronegative groups of the organic pollutants were easily adsorbed on the carbonized NCDs, and the adsorbed molecule underwent oxidation on the surface of carbonized NCDs/PbO2 electrode. This results in a rapid decomposition of organic pollutants over the present carbonized NCDs/PbO2 electrode.

In this work, NCDs-T/PbO2 electrode was prepared by electrodeposition method, and its electrocatalytic performance was studied via degradation of methylene blue. XRD characterization results showed that the characteristic peak of α-PbO2 disappeared and converted into β-PbO2 with the introduction of NCDs. The results of the electrochemical tests showed that the construction of the NCDs-T intermediate layer can make the electrode impedance decrease from 392.9 Ω to 7.71 Ω, and the OEP of the electrode increased from 1.77 V to 1.86 V. Besides, only a slight decrease (5 %) of degradation efficiency was observed for the NCDs-600/PbO2 electrode after 10 cycles, indicating that the introduction of the NCDs-600 intermediate layer has enhanced the stability of the electrode. In addition, the TOC removal rate was found up to 50.3 % together with 4.06 % of the mineralization efficiency. Furthermore, the degradation process was in line with first kinetic law. When the degradation process was carried out under the optimal conditions (MB solution of 100 mg L −1, the initial pH of 5, and the current density of 30 mA cm−2), the degradation rate of methylene blue by the NCDs-800/PbO2 electrode was 91.6 %. Additionally, the enhanced electrocatalytic mechanism was further unveiled by DFT calculations.

Wenlong Liu: Writing – original draft. Yuanfeng Wu: Writing – review & editing, Software. Guiyun Yi: Supervision. Xiangkun Guo: Supervision. Yue Yin: Investigation, Data curation. Bin Xu: Supervision. Jinfang Zhang: Data curation. Lutong Sun: Data curation. Huihui Zeng: Supervision. Baolin Xing: Supervision. Yulong Zhang: Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: No conflict of internet exists in the submission of this manuscript, and it is approved by all authors for publication, I would like to declare on behalf of my coauthors that the work of described is original research that has not been published previously, and not under consideration for publication elsewhere, in whole or in part.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52304284), Fundamental Research Funds for the Universities of Henan Province (No. NSFRF240609), GHfund B (No. 202407022319), Doctoral Fund of Henan Polytechnic University (B2022-44), and the Institute of Zhejiang University-Quzhou (IZQ2022RCZX007, IZQ2023KJ1013). We also appreciate the Big Data Center of Henan Polytechnic University for providing the facility support on the numerical calculations in this work.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2024.139453.
